k115: Three things we can do that others can't
k116: We integrate four signals at once—identity events, session footage, in-app content and data flow—forming a proprietary trust dataset that grows richer with use. This is the shared foundation of our three differentiators.
k124: “In the past 7 days, did anyone here paste customer data into ChatGPT?”
k125: 69% of organizations suspect employees use unauthorized GenAI tools; the average company runs 14 AI tools but IT knows only 4–5. Truly governing Shadow AI takes 'two hands' working together—the first on the employee endpoint, seeing the clipboard and which LLM the browser reaches; the second at the traffic choke point, forcing access to approved models only through the enterprise Gateway. Endpoint-only DLP can see but not control; network-only DLP can control but not see. Sanctward is the only vendor with both.
k134: “How many AI agents do we actually have? Who owns them? If something breaks, can we find anyone?”
k135: MCP, function-calling and autonomous workflows mean every company will soon have 'more AI agents than employees.' Yet most of these non-human identities are ghosts—no owner, no permission boundary, no audit trail. Sanctward treats every AI agent as a governable 'employee': issuing a verifiable identity passport (private_key_jwt / Client Credentials), binding an owner, scoping the systems and data it can access, auditing everything, locking on anomalies and revoking with one click. We define the standard for this new category first—followers are 2–3 years behind.
k139: “Give me, for the last 90 days, all evidence of schema changes to the production database.”
k140: Traditional recording is a liability—hours of footage to scrub through afterward. Sanctward turns every session recording into a searchable 'Google for actions.' Endpoint-side recording, OCR full-text indexing and WORM tamper-proof storage let you pinpoint exactly when any action happened—and the session clip—with a single natural-language query. Auditors get a ready-made evidence pack mapped to the clauses, not a pile of raw logs—cutting SOC 2 evidence prep from a week to 30 minutes.
k145: Replace legacy-architecture headaches with zero-trust answers
k146: From VPN and remote access to identity and compliance—six of the most common scenarios, covered by one Sanctward platform.
k148: From 'opening the whole network' to 'opening only authorized apps'
k149: Once you're on a traditional SSL VPN, the entire internal network is wide open to you—and there's no visibility into what happens inside the encrypted tunnel. That's the most common gateway for lateral movement and data leakage.
k151: Over-trust: once connected, you can move laterally to any internal host.
k152: No content visibility: actions inside the encrypted tunnel can't be monitored in real time.
k153: No endpoint control: access is granted without checking device compliance, so stolen credentials walk right in.
k155: Zero-trust per-app authorization; backend locations are hidden, so there's no 'internal network' to traverse.

k173: Persistent endpoint tunnels bypass enterprise controls—who connected and what they did can't be seen or investigated.

Sanctward lets admins instantly share any live RDP / VNC / SSH / Telnet session and switch seamlessly from 'watching' to 'taking over'—the whole process recorded and instantly indexed for search.

k202: Each system has its own accounts and its own MFA (or none)—management is fragmented, audits have blind spots, and cleanly offboarding accounts in one shot is even harder.
k203: Scattered accounts: a separate credential per system per employee—resets and reclaiming are slow and error-prone for IT.
k204: Inconsistent MFA: some TOTP, some SMS, some none—poor experience and lingering risk.
k205: Legacy systems don't support SSO / MFA and stay outside unified control for the long term.

k220: Taiwan Cyber Security Act · PDPA · FSC, aligned at once
k221: Taiwan's Cyber Security Management Act and its tiered regulations, the Personal Data Protection Act, and the FSC's remote-maintenance requirements all set clear rules for log retention, access control and audit trails. Sanctward maps directly to these clauses, and its compliance adapter auto-generates auditable evidence.
k223: The Tiered Cyber Security Responsibility regulations require logs kept ≥ 6 months, recording account/permission changes, logins, IPs and data access → 100+ audit events + WORM immutability + Merkle evidence chain, with configurable retention.
k224: High/medium responsibility tiers require MFA and strict access control for critical systems → built-in IdP with consistent MFA (TOTP), fine-grained RBAC, conditional access, instant offboarding.
k225: FSC remote-maintenance / outsourcing audits require connection records and recording → full RDP / SSH / VNC / Telnet recording + OCR command search + controlled credential injection + JIT time-boxing.
k226: PDPA Article 27 security measures and access trails → end-to-end logging of access to personal-data systems, automatic masking of sensitive fields, fully traceable evidence.
k238: Going global means meeting international frameworks' access-control, audit-logging and incident-response requirements. Sanctward's controls already map directly to the specific clauses of ISO 27001:2022, NIST 800-207 and Japan's APPI, generating an evidence pack in one click; SOC 2, GDPR, NIS2 and HIPAA mappings are coming soon.
k239: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 A.8.5 secure authentication, A.8.15 logging, A.8.16 monitoring, A.5.15 access control → unified SSO / MFA, 100+ event logs, real-time monitoring and trust scoring.
k240: NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture: continuous verification, per-session dynamic authorization, trust algorithm → trust-gateway per-app authorization + real-time 0–100 trust score + continuous re-verification.
k241: Japan APPI (Act on Protection of Personal Information): security control measures, access control and audit trails → full audit trail on personal-data access, automatic masking of sensitive fields, Merkle-immutable auditing.
k242: Coming soon — SOC 2 Trust Services (CC6 access control, CC7 monitoring & incident response): underlying controls (RBAC + MFA, WORM audit trail, behavioral-baseline anomaly detection) are in place; formal clause mapping is coming soon.
k243: Coming soon — EU GDPR (Art. 32), EU NIS2 (Art. 21), US HIPAA (§164.312): access control, audit logging and incident response already overlap and are in place; clause mappings are rolling out.

metaTitle: Sanctward — The Enterprise Trust OS for the AI Era
e1why: With a VPN, one stolen credential is a pass to the entire internal network—attackers move laterally between hosts and widen the damage, and most major breaches involve exactly this lateral movement. Zero trust's 'containerized access' limits every connection to a single app, so even a compromised endpoint can't spread—this is what shrinks an incident's blast radius.
e1how: Zero trust runs on 'never trust, always verify': users don't connect to a network segment—they go through a trust gateway that authorizes access to specific apps based on identity, device posture and context, re-checked continuously. Backends sit behind the gateway with no inbound ports, drastically reducing the attack surface.
e2why: Exposing RDP and similar ports to the internet is one of the most common ransomware entry points, while third-party remote tools open persistent channels on endpoints that IT can't see—bypassing every control. Without recording and command auditing, privileged sessions can't answer 'who did what' after an incident, nor satisfy FSC, PCI DSS or SOX audits.
e2how: A controlled gateway becomes the single entry point for all remote protocols: connections and credential injection happen server-side (users never touch passwords), the whole session is proxied and recorded, and backend ports stay fully closed. Every action is captured as a searchable, tamper-proof record.
e3why: Senior-engineer assistance, cross-team collaboration, onboarding and live audit monitoring all need everyone to 'see the same screen.' Separate logins are inefficient and blur accountability and the activity trail—and make it impossible to step in during an emergency.
e3how: Session sharing streams a live session to multiple viewers in real time; when needed, 'watching' switches to 'taking over' in one click so authorized staff can act directly. Everything is recorded and OCR-indexed in real time—who watched, who took over and what they did is all logged and searchable.
e4why: The more scattered accounts are, the larger the attack surface—and the more likely offboarded accounts become 'orphan accounts' that turn into breaches; inconsistent MFA leaves gaps too. Unified identity makes 'who can access what' centralized, controllable and auditable: disable once and access dies everywhere, while improving UX and cutting password-reset load.
e4how: A built-in IdP acts as the single authentication authority: modern apps use OIDC/SAML federation while legacy apps are onboarded via form-fill or reverse proxy—covering 90%+ of auth modes. Directory sync with AD/LDAP automates the account lifecycle, with risk-based step-up MFA.
e5why: Compliance isn't optional: Taiwan's Cyber Security Management Act, the Personal Data Protection Act and FSC rules impose clear obligations on log retention, access control and auditing—violations risk fines and reputational damage. The real difficulty is that gathering evidence by hand is slow, and proving logs are complete and untampered is hard.
e5how: The compliance adapter automatically maps platform audit events to specific clauses; WORM immutability plus a Merkle evidence chain guarantee integrity and verifiability, and one click produces a clause-mapped evidence pack—cutting audit prep from weeks to tens of minutes.
e6why: Going global means customers and regulators expect ISO 27001, NIST 800-207, Japan APPI, plus SOC 2, GDPR, NIS2 and HIPAA. The good news: these frameworks' underlying controls overlap heavily—access control, audit logging, incident response—so what matters is mapping to each one's clauses and producing verifiable evidence.
e6how: Sanctward maps one set of controls (unified identity/MFA, session recording, immutable logs, real-time monitoring) to each framework's clauses. Clause mapping and evidence-pack generation are available today for ISO 27001:2022, NIST 800-207 and Japan APPI; SOC 2 / GDPR / NIS2 / HIPAA are coming soon.
c1ctx: A 500-person manufacturer runs a dozen-plus systems—SAP/MES, Zabbix, QNAP, FortiGate, GitLab—with only 5 IT staff. Each system has its own accounts and outside vendors come and go for maintenance, leaving identity and remote-access management nearly crushing the IT team.
c2ctx: Branches plus core banking systems, with vendors stationed long-term doing remote maintenance. Under the FSC's remote-maintenance audit requirements, 'what the vendor did on the system' must be fully reconstructable—yet legacy VPN+RDP can't enforce recording, and passwords are known to almost everyone.
c3ctx: A tech company that's fully remote, globally distributed and heavily outsourced—with no trusted 'internal network' perimeter. The CISO mandates zero trust: every access checks identity, device and behavior, and contractor access must be granted fast and reliably revoked.
c4ctx: An MSP runs a self-built system serving 30 enterprise customers, each demanding environment isolation and data sovereignty. Maintaining a separate account and control stack per customer would make ops cost balloon linearly with customer count.
utpHeading: UTP-Ai — the Unified Trust Platform, driven by AI
utpIntro: Sanctward isn't a bundle of separate security tools—it's one AI-driven Unified Trust Platform. Three ideas capture the spirit of the whole system:
utpU_p: Identity, zero trust, privileged access, endpoints and compliance—integrated on one signal chain, one console and one data-sovereignty model, not five disconnected products.
utpT_p: Zero trust by default: trust no preset environment—every connection is scored in real time and continuously verified to allow, step up, or block.
utpAi_p: AI is the engine that binds it all together—and the core spirit: it reads four signals at once (identity, behavior, in-app content and data flow), seeing what people do, what AI agents do behind the scenes, and where data is going. This ever-richer signal dataset is a moat no one can replicate.
utpSpirit: is a starting point — the whole architecture is designed to be the engine that makes 'unified' and 'trust' real
d1why: Of all the data employees paste into public AI, source code leaks the most; once it enters a public model it may train future versions—handing over trade secrets and IP. And it happens at the 'browser and clipboard' layer, where traditional network or endpoint tools often can't see or stop it.
d1how: Real control needs 'two hands': an endpoint agent that sees the clipboard and which LLM the browser reaches, and an enterprise Gateway as a traffic choke point that inspects and blocks content before it reaches an external LLM—while offering an approved enterprise-LLM path. Interlocked, they let you both see and control.
d2why: MCP, function-calling and autonomous workflows mean companies will soon have 'more AI agents than employees.' Without an owner, permission boundary or audit trail, these non-human identities are unaccountable ghosts—if abused or hijacked, no one notices and no one is responsible.
d2how: Treat every AI agent as a governable 'employee': issue a verifiable identity passport via private_key_jwt / Client Credentials, bind an owner, scope the systems and data it can touch, audit everything, lock on anomalies and revoke in one click—so non-human identities are governed just like people.
d3why: Traditional session recording is a liability: after an incident you scrub hours of video to find the key moment, and audits take weeks to assemble evidence that's hard to prove complete and untampered. Recorded but unsearchable and unprovable is as good as not recorded.
d3how: Turn every recording into a 'Google for operations': endpoint-side recording, OCR full-text indexing and WORM tamper-proof storage let one natural-language query pinpoint the exact moment and clip of any action—auditors get a ready-made evidence pack mapped to the clauses.
pf_h1: The platform’s five capabilities, in depth
pf_intro: Sanctward integrates five capabilities—identity, zero trust, privileged access, endpoints and compliance—on one platform and one signal chain. Below, each capability is explained as it actually works in Sanctward, with real-world examples.
pf_chain_h: One signal chain: how the five connect
pf_chain_p: This isn't five products bolted together—it's one data flow: identity feeds the trust score, device posture affects it in real time, the score drives every access decision, and every action is written to immutable audit.
pf_iam_eg: Employee Chen signs in to SAP, GitLab and the company QNAP with one account; even a legacy system with no SSO interface is onboarded via form autofill. The day Chen leaves, AD sync auto-disables the account—access dies across every system at once, with no manual cleanup.
pf_pam_eg: A contractor needs to maintain the production database. They file a JIT request (purpose and duration); after manager approval they get 2 hours of temporary access. The session is fully recorded, the password is injected by the system (never shown to the contractor), and when time's up access drops to zero automatically. To check 'what commands they ran,' an OCR search for 'DROP TABLE' jumps to that exact frame in seconds.
pf_zta_eg: Alice usually connects from Taipei during work hours—trust score 92, allowed. One night at 03:14 a login appears from Tokyo: the system flags 'impossible travel' plus off-hours, and the score drops to 48—triggering step-up MFA, or a disconnect if still suspicious. The whole decision happens in milliseconds and is invisible to Alice during normal use.
pf_ep_eg: IT pushes the installer to every laptop via GPO/MDM. Auto-approval rules (e.g., Windows 11 22H2 and joined to the acme domain) approve matching devices automatically—~99% hands-free, the rest queued for review. When one laptop's motherboard is swapped, its fingerprint changes → the system detects 'fingerprint drift' and asks an admin to re-verify before allowing it.
pf_ca_eg: Audit season arrives and the auditor wants 'all permission changes in the last 90 days.' The admin filters by event type in one click; each entry carries a before/after delta (who changed whose permission from what to what, plus IP and time), proven untampered by the Merkle evidence chain, and a clause-mapped, digitally signed PDF evidence report is produced in one click—cutting prep from weeks to tens of minutes.
pf_iam_mfa: Free built-in MFA (TOTP two-factor), unlimited accounts at no extra cost—even if a password leaks, an attacker without the second factor can't sign in.
pf_idp_how: Sanctward is a complete OIDC IdP: an app just enters the Discovery URL plus a Client ID/Secret to hand login to Sanctward, making it the company's single sign-on hub (RS256/ES256 signing, custom claims, M2M, token TTLs).
pf_mfa_oidc: It can also act as a Service Provider to external identities: auth gateways connect any OIDC/SAML/LDAP, and RADIUS-over-OIDC folds existing hardware-token / push MFA into one login flow.
pf_m2m: For machine-to-machine (M2M): backend services, CI/CD or scheduled jobs get an access token via the client_credentials flow (Client ID/Secret or private_key_jwt), protected by IP allowlists and rate limits, with M2M-specific claims injected—no humans, no scattered passwords.
idpSecH: Three ways the built-in IdP works
idpSecP: One OIDC identity engine powers three scenarios: user single sign-on, third-party MFA federation, and machine-to-machine (M2M). Each relationship diagram below walks through the requests and responses between roles.
idpB_h: Mode 2 — Third-party MFA / IdP (Sanctward as SP)
pp_intro: SANCTWARD CO., LTD. (“we”, “us”) respects and protects your personal data. This policy explains how we collect, use and safeguard personal data on this website, in accordance with Taiwan’s Personal Data Protection Act and other applicable laws.
pp_s1: When you submit the Book-a-Demo or contact form, we collect the name, company/organization, email and message you provide; on submission we also record your source IP address, approximate location and network organization to identify prospective-customer origin, prevent abuse and support security auditing. While you browse, we may collect anonymous technical information (such as browser type and page interactions) to operate and improve the site.
pp_s2: We use your information only to respond to enquiries, arrange product demos, provide information you request, and operate and improve the website. We do not use it for unrelated purposes.
pp_s3: This website may use essential cookies and aggregate analytics to understand overall usage. You can refuse non-essential cookies via your browser settings, which may affect some features.
pp_s4: We do not sell your personal data. We share it only when necessary to provide our services, with your consent, or where required by law—with processors or authorities bound to protect it.
pp_s5: We retain your data only for as long as necessary to fulfil the purposes of collection, and apply appropriate technical and organizational safeguards against unauthorized access, alteration or disclosure.
pp_s6: Under the Personal Data Protection Act, you may request to access, review, obtain copies of, correct or delete your personal data, and to stop its collection, processing or use. To exercise these rights, contact us below.
pp_s7: If you have any questions about this Privacy Policy or wish to exercise your rights, email us and we’ll respond promptly.
cf_sent: Sent — we'll get back to you shortly.
cf_error: Couldn't send. Please try again later, or email gary.ho@sanctward.com directly.
iae_h1: Your identity hub — OIDC, SAML, MFA and RADIUS in one engine
iae_intro: Sanctward IAE is a built-in authentication engine: one identity core that acts as an identity provider, federates your existing identity sources, and guards the front door with phishing-resistant passwordless login. New apps, legacy systems and cloud all converge into one secure, auditable login flow.
iae_roleA_d: Let any of your apps log in with a Sanctward account — both OIDC and SAML, plus a reverse-proxy sub-mode so even legacy systems without modern login get SSO.
iae_roleB_d: Let users sign in with existing Google, Microsoft or AD identities via OIDC, SAML, RADIUS and LDAP gateways, with automatic provisioning and group mapping.
iae_roleC_d: Guard logins with phishing-resistant Passkeys, TOTP and Email OTP — eliminating credential theft and relay attacks — as primary login or second factor.
iae_proto_h: OIDC, SAML, MFA, RADIUS — all built in
iae_proto_p: One protocol-agnostic identity core: any upstream identity source connects to any downstream app. Here are the core capabilities of each protocol.
iae_oidc_d: A production-ready, complete OpenID Connect provider with per-tenant signing.
iae_oidc_1: Full endpoints: Discovery, JWKS, Authorize, Token, UserInfo, Introspect, Revoke
iae_oidc_2: Authorization code, refresh (auto-rotation) and client credentials (M2M) grants
iae_oidc_3: Relay reverse-proxy sub-mode: SSO for legacy apps, tokens never reach the browser
iae_oidc_4: M2M security suite: IP allowlist, rate limiting, anomaly detection, lockout, private_key_jwt replay protection
iae_saml_d: Issue signed assertions as an IdP, or consume external SAML sources as an SP.
iae_saml_1: SAML IdP: signed assertions, IdP metadata, SP- and IdP-initiated flows
iae_saml_3: One-click AWS console federation — no per-user IAM accounts
iae_saml_4: Per-tenant X.509 signing cert, issued by the tenant's own Root CA
iae_mfa_d: Phishing-resistant strong auth, built into every login flow.
iae_mfa_1: Passkey / FIDO2 (Windows Hello, Touch ID, YubiKey), TOTP, Email OTP
iae_mfa_2: Step-up on federated login, prompting re-verification by risk
iae_mfa_3: External MFA verify API (Duo-style): your app owns primary login, calls Sanctward for the second factor
iae_radius_d: Let RADIUS-only legacy systems use Sanctward as their auth server.
iae_radius_1: Standard RADIUS server: credential auth + returned attributes (VSA and attribute catalog)
iae_radius_2: MFA Access-Challenge: add a second factor even over RADIUS
iae_radius_3: CoA zero-trust disconnect: drop a live session the moment posture degrades
iae_radius_4: RadSec encrypted transport, automatic per-tenant port allocation
iae_diff_1d: SSO for apps that don't speak OIDC, with tokens never exposed to the front end — most IdPs can't.
iae_diff_2d: Allowlist + rate limiting + anomaly detection + replay protection out of the box; most open-source IdPs make you assemble it.
iae_diff_3d: A protocol-agnostic core: any upstream to any downstream — a paid add-on elsewhere, near-effortless here.
iae_diff_4d: True multi-tenant isolation, not one shared key — a natural fit for MSP delivery across many customers.
iae_mat_p: All of the following are shipped as of June 2026; the only item still on the roadmap is RADIUS EAP-TLS.
iae_mat_2: SAML IdP / SP, SLO, encrypted assertions
iae_mat_4: RADIUS auth, MFA challenge, returned attributes, CoA disconnect, RadSec
iae_mat_5: External federation: OIDC / SAML / RADIUS / LDAP
iae_uc_h: One identity core, four ways to land
iae_uc_1d: One identity for every app; with passwordless login, no passwords to type ever again.
iae_uc_2d: Staff reach the AWS console, Salesforce and Workday with their corporate identity — no per-user accounts.
iae_uc_3d: VPNs and network gear that only speak RADIUS authenticate with Sanctward credentials + MFA.
iae_uc_4d: Back-end services obtain controlled tokens via client_credentials, protected by IP limits, rate limiting and anomaly detection.
sso_h1: Log in once, securely reach every app
sso_intro: With one identity core, Sanctward lets staff sign in to every company system with a single account — including 22 built-in app templates and reverse-proxy login that brings even legacy systems without a modern login under management, plus full account, group and permission control.
sso_pil_h: Modern to legacy — ~90% of auth scenarios covered
sso_p1_d: Modern apps connect over standard protocols, or let Sanctward act as the IdP for company-wide SSO.
sso_p2_d: proxy_vault stores credentials encrypted and autofills forms; header injection or pass-through proxy brings legacy systems in without modification.
sso_p3_d: Visual header/form/cookie mapping covers ~80% of cases; complex ones use Lua (sso_helpers SDK).
sso_cap_h: Identity, directory and permissions in one place
sso_cap_p: From directory sync to fine-grained authorization, the account lifecycle stays controlled and auditable end to end.
sso_c1_b1: WordPress, FortiGate, QNAP, OWA, GitLab, SAP… one-click apply
sso_c1_b3: Clone a built-in to customize, or save an existing app back as a template
sso_c2_b1: Attribute mapping, group mapping, JIT provisioning
sso_c2_b2: Auto-disable on departure, auto-restore on return
sso_c2_b3: Connection test, filter test, attribute preview tools
sso_c3_b1: Nested groups, role inheritance, CSV import/export
sso_c3_b2: App assignment with conditions (IP / time / MFA)
sso_c3_b3: Password policy, account expiry, bulk actions
sso_c4_b3: Lockout on failed login, IP/UA fingerprint binding
sso_u1_d: One account logs into SAP, GitLab and QNAP; on the day they leave, AD sync auto-disables them everywhere at once.
sso_u2_d: Converge access across Google, AD, SaaS and internal systems into one controlled, auditable hub.
sso_u3_d: Firewalls, mail and NAS without an SSO interface join single sign-on via reverse-proxy autofill.
pam_h1: Record, govern and auto-revoke every privileged session
pam_intro: Sanctward delivers secure remote access from the browser, fully recorded and OCR-searchable, with just-in-time authorization and clipboard DLP — so high-risk contractor and ops access happens in a controlled, auditable environment.
pam_pil_h: Clientless, plus a high-performance native client
pam_p1_d: Pure browser operation on Guacamole; multi-monitor, audio, disk and printer redirection.
pam_p2_d: Windows/macOS/Linux, H.264 + QUIC dual channel, <15ms LAN latency, 300–500 sessions per core.
pam_p3_d: Live monitoring, force disconnect, multi-user shadow takeover, auto-disconnect on time limit.
pam_cap_p: From just-in-time grants to content audit, high-risk operations stay scoped to the minimum.
pam_c1_b3: Supports emergency access and contractor flows
pam_c2_b1: Endpoint-side recording, intact even under E2E encryption
pam_c2_b3: OCR full-text search jumps straight to the moment
pam_c3_b1: Allow / warn / block / large-paste — four classes
pam_c3_b2: Built-in rules for card numbers, SSN, source code + custom
pam_c4_b1: Inject credentials after approval; user never sees plaintext
pam_c4_b2: Posture Lock auto-locks a session when the device is non-compliant
pam_u1_d: Let vendors operate under recording and DLP monitoring, with every action searchable afterwards.
pam_u2_d: Temporary privilege via JIT request and approval, auto-revoked on countdown — zero residue.
pam_u3_d: RDP recording and command parsing for regulator remote-ops audit, out of the box.
zta_h1: Prove trust on every connection; downgrade non-compliant devices on the spot
zta_intro: Sanctward drives continuous verification with a trust score, combined with device posture reported by endpoint agents, to decide every access dynamically — and a full endpoint fleet manager onboards thousands of devices and renews certificates automatically.
zta_p1_d: Geo-IP, behavioral inertia, impossible travel, device posture and threat intel weighted into a 0–100 score.
zta_p2_d: Score >90 allow, 60–90 step-up MFA, <60 deny — compared in sub-seconds.
zta_p3_d: If the score drops mid-session, trigger re-verification or force disconnect.
zta_cap_h: From device attestation to immutable audit
zta_cap_p: The full Z0–Z11 zero-trust path, paired with whole-lifecycle endpoint fleet management.
zta_c1_b3: Posture checks: OS version, BitLocker/FileVault, EDR
zta_c4_b2: Device swap, revival, remote removal flows
zta_uc_h: Trust isn't one-time — it's every time
zta_u1_d: Access to internal systems from any network is allowed or stepped up by live trust score.
zta_u2_d: Devices with BitLocker off or EDR anomalies are downgraded or locked automatically.
zta_u3_d: When posture degrades mid-session, re-verify or disconnect instantly.
compliance_h1: Turn daily audit logs into evidence auditors can trust, in one click
compliance_intro: Sanctward's built-in compliance reporting engine maps every login, access, privileged approval and device-posture event to regulatory controls across regions and industries, producing bilingual, tenant-CA-signed, Merkle-tamper-proof audit-grade reports — no extra GRC tool, no manual log digging.
compliance_pil_h: Pick a framework and period, generate in seconds
compliance_p1_d: Each control is marked covered / partial / gap, with exceptions and remediation listed automatically.
compliance_p2_d: Every item is SHA-256 hashed with a Merkle root for the whole report; alter one character and it's detected.
compliance_p3_d: One-click EN/中 switch; cross-border reports can enable PII masking with stable subject aliases.
compliance_cap_h: Not a marketing doc — audit evidence
compliance_cap_p: A 13-section structure aligned with international audit-report conventions, from cover to evidence integrity.
compliance_c1_b1: Each report signed by the tenant's own Root CA (ECDSA P-256)
compliance_c1_b2: Merkle root proves the evidence set wasn't cherry-picked or altered
compliance_c2_b2: SSCA maturity, FSC, Taiwan Cyber Security Act, APPI
compliance_c2_b3: A new regulation = a new mapping file, no code change
compliance_c3_b2: Exceptions & gaps, remediation tracking, control evidence detail
compliance_c3_b3: Management assertion, CUEC appendix, evidence integrity
compliance_c4_b2: Proactively flags uncovered and partially covered items
compliance_uc_h: No more overtime digging logs at audit time
compliance_u1_d: Pick a framework and period; generate a verifiable audit-grade report in seconds.
compliance_u2_d: One dataset, one-click EN/中, optional PII masking across borders.
compliance_u3_d: Differentiated frameworks for finance (FSC) and semiconductors (SSCA), all included.
reports_h1: The platform's single reporting hub — pick, scope, generate in seconds
reports_intro: Sanctward abstracts reporting into a platform-level horizontal capability (Report Engine + provider registry): there is exactly one place to generate reports, each report type plugs in as a provider, and Compliance is the first one.
reports_p1_d: The platform's only report hub; feature pages keep just read-only dashboards and deep links.
reports_p2_d: Compliance is the first provider; user, app, audit and authorization reports extend as providers.
reports_p3_d: Pick framework → pick period → generate PDF, scope-aware with tenant isolation.
reports_cap_h: From generation to delivery, end to end
reports_cap_p: The report engine has scheduling, delivery, signing, masking and access control built in.
reports_c1_b1: Pick framework and period, generate PDF in seconds
reports_c2_b1: Scheduler daemon generates on a cadence
reports_c4_b1: A new report type = a new provider, no reinvention
reports_c4_b3: User / app / authorization reports on the roadmap
reports_u1_d: All reports come from one entry with consistent format and governance.
reports_u2_d: Monthly audit reports generate and email to owners automatically.
reports_u3_d: Add user, app and authorization operational reports as providers over time.
sso_dg_h: Log in once, bridge new and legacy
compliance_dg_h: From audit events to verifiable reports
reports_dg_h: One engine, many reports, central delivery
pf_rpt_p: The platform's single reporting entry: a horizontal report engine plus a provider ecosystem — pick a framework and period, generate a verifiable report in seconds.
pf_rpt_1: Report Engine + provider registry, Compliance as the first provider
pf_rpt_2: Scheduled auto-generation, delivery to email / SFTP / NFS
pf_rpt_3: Tenant-CA signature, Merkle tamper-proofing, PII masking, RBAC
pf_rpt_eg: An audit owner schedules an ISO 27001 report on the 1st each month; the system signs, seals and emails it. User and authorization reports extend on the same engine later.
rad_p: Bring RADIUS-only network gear and legacy systems onto the same identity core, the same MFA and the same audit trail — and upgrade one-time login into continuous in-session control.
rad_app1_d: Authenticate to VPN gateways with Sanctward credentials and native MFA — no separate user store to maintain.
rad_app2_d: Centralized admin login for switches, firewalls and AP consoles — no per-device local accounts.
rad_app3_d: Auto-return VLAN and ACL attributes (Tunnel-Private-Group-Id, Filter-Id) by role/department at login for instant segmentation.
rad_app4_d: RFC 2866 accounting (Start/Interim/Stop): who, from where, how long, how much — merged into one audit timeline.
rad_app5_d: Evaluate posture and trust live; non-compliant or low-trust sessions are dropped mid-stream via CoA, not at the next login.
rad_app6_d: Per-tenant auto port allocation plus RadSec SNI routing — serve many customers from one cloud with zero shared-secret leakage.
rad_fa_p: From the NAS sending credentials to Access-Accept with network attributes — all on the same MFA engine as OIDC/SAML.
rad_fb_p: Traditional NAC checks once at login; Sanctward keeps watching and disconnects the moment posture drops.
rad_b2: Trigger: posture fail / low trust / JIT expiry
rad_b4: NAS drops the session, revokes IP / VLAN
rad_b5: Log event; user can re-auth once remediated
mfa_p: MFA as a horizontal layer: passwordless login, OIDC/SAML step-up, an external verification API, and RADIUS/VPN challenge — all sharing the same factors and audit trail.
mfa_app1_d: Replace passwords with Passkey/FIDO2: credentials are bound to your domain and the private key never leaves the device — phishing- and credential-theft-resistant by cryptography.
mfa_app2_d: Downstream apps declare the assurance they need via acr_values / RequestedAuthnContext; if the session is too weak, Sanctward triggers step-up MFA on the spot.
mfa_app3_d: Duo-style: third-party apps keep their own primary login and call /api/mfa/v1 for the 2nd factor, getting an allow/deny verdict — with client creds, IP allowlist and rate limiting.
mfa_app4_d: With RADIUS Access-Challenge enabled, network gear and VPN logins can require TOTP/Email OTP — adding a second factor to legacy devices with no rewrite.
mfa_app5_d: The trust engine scores each login (geo-IP, behavior baseline, device trust): low risk skips MFA, medium challenges, high denies — pluggable into both internal and API flows.
mfa_app6_d: Users register a passkey, enable TOTP and receive Email OTP in account settings and can revoke them; admins can override, disable or reset enrollment.
mfa_fa_p: The user signs in passwordless with a passkey; when a downstream app demands higher assurance, Sanctward adds the second factor before issuing the token/assertion.
mfa_a2: Redirect to IAE with required acr
mfa_a5: Verify signature, match acr, build session
mfa_fb_p: The third-party app keeps its own primary login and hands only the second factor to Sanctward; verification is fail-closed — any error denies access.
mfa_b3: Look up factor, open 120s tx, send OTP
mfa_b4: Prompt the user for the code
iae_mat_6: Protocol bridging (SAML↔OIDC, RADIUS→OIDC/SAML, all e2e green)
brg_h: Protocol Bridging: come in one way, go out another
brg_p: Authenticate with any upstream protocol; Sanctward builds a protocol-neutral identity session that any downstream issuer reuses — with no dedicated ‘bridge’ configuration.
brg_app1_d: Use an existing SAML IdP identity to sign in to OIDC-only modern apps — no new accounts.
brg_app2_d: Use an OIDC/social identity to reach SAML-only legacy SaaS (e.g. AWS Console).
brg_app3_d: Turn a network-layer RADIUS federated identity into downstream OIDC/SAML web SSO.
brg_app4_d: A protocol-neutral EndUser session is built after auth and reused by any downstream issuer.
brg_app5_d: No separate ‘bridge’ screen: set up the upstream gateway plus the downstream issuer and it just works.
brg_app6_d: Any upstream (OIDC RP / SAML SP / RADIUS) × any downstream (OIDC / SAML / RADIUS).
brg_fa_p: Any upstream consumer × any downstream issuer — all converging on one neutral identity session.
brg_fb_p: The user signs in with an external SAML identity yet ends up with the OIDC token the downstream app needs.
brg_b6: Signed in: identity from SAML, token is OIDC
zta_zt_h: The Zero-Trust Decision Flow for Every Access
zta_zt_p: From request to signal scoring to a three-way decision and continuous in-session re-verification — trust is never assumed; it is re-proven every time.
zta_zt_n2_s: Geo-IP · behavior · impossible travel · posture · intel
zta_mfa_h: MFA wired into every zero-trust decision
zta_mfa_p: MFA isn’t a one-time login step; it’s triggered dynamically by the trust score — using the same phishing-resistant factor engine as IAE.
zta_mfa_c1_b1: A 60–90 trust score requires a second factor right then
zta_mfa_c1_b2: Pass to proceed; fail denies the connection
zta_mfa_c1_b3: Low risk (>90) is uninterrupted; high risk (<60) is denied
zta_mfa_c2_b3: Uses the same MFA engine as IAE
zta_mfa_c3_b1: If the score drops mid-session, MFA is required again
zta_mfa_c3_b3: Linked to device posture and behavior baseline
zta_mfa_c4_b1: The same MFA decision applies to OIDC/SAML login
zta_mfa_c4_b3: Every step-up event lands in one audit trail
zta_mfa_s6: Keep scoring in-session; drop → re-verify / disconnect
